title: System Security & Hardware RoT
description: Hardware trust, secure boot, and physical security requirements for humanoid robots.

items.text: Hardware Root of Trust (eFuse, TPM 2.0, TEE)
items.description: All security operations must be based on a unique identifier and keys anchored in hardware (eFuse, TPM 2.0, or TEE).

items.text: Chain of Trust Booting (ROM to RootFS)
items.description: A verifiable chain must be established where each stage of the boot process (ROM -> Bootloader -> Kernel -> RootFS) is cryptographically signed and verified before execution.
The system must support the use of developer-specific keys to prevent the use of generic vendor default keys, ensuring unique identity per manufacturer/model.
Implement hardware-backed security counters to prevent attackers from downgrading the firmware to an older, vulnerable version.
Physically disable or cryptographically lock hardware debug interfaces (JTAG, UART) on production units to prevent physical memory dumping or shell access.
The primary root filesystem must be mounted as read-only to prevent persistent malware installation or unauthorized configuration changes.
Enable kernel-level protections including Address Space Layout Randomization (KASLR), Control Flow Integrity (CFI), and Stack Canaries.
Ensure all robot processes run with the minimum required privileges using distinct UID/GID for each functional module (e.g., perception, navigation, control).
Use Linux cgroups to strictly limit the CPU, memory, and I/O resources available to non-critical processes, preventing resource exhaustion attacks.
The system should have no default open ports or unnecessary services enabled out-of-the-box, following the 'Secure by Default' principle.
All data stored on internal storage (SSD/eMMC) must be encrypted using AES-256, protecting sensitive AI models and logs from physical theft.
Encryption keys must be bound to the hardware state (PCRs in TPM), ensuring data can only be decrypted if the system hasn't been tampered with.
Remove all symbol tables from production binaries and apply code obfuscation to critical algorithms to protect intellectual property.
All cryptographic keys must be generated, stored, and used within a Hardware Secure World (TEE/HSM), never exposed to the main OS memory.
Equip the robot chassis with physical sensors to detect unauthorized opening, triggering an immediate security alert.
Upon detection of a critical physical breach or tampering attempt, the system must automatically zeroize (erase) all sensitive cryptographic keys.
Implement a kernel-level whitelist for authorized USB/Ethernet devices, blocking any unauthorized peripheral connections.

title: Ubuntu OS & Kernel Hardening
description: OS-level security specifications for Ubuntu environment.

items.description: Enable Kernel Address Space Layout Randomization (KASLR) to prevent attackers from predicting the memory addresses of critical kernel functions.
Enforce Privileged Execute Never (PXN) and Privileged Access Never (PAN) to block the kernel from executing or accessing user-space memory.
Compile the kernel and all system binaries with stack canaries to detect and prevent stack buffer overflow attacks.
Configure critical kernel data structures as read-only after initialization to prevent runtime tampering by exploits.
Apply strict AppArmor profiles to all ROS 2 nodes to restrict their access to only necessary files, network ports, and system resources.
Ensure all robot services and user-space applications run as non-root users, minimizing the impact of a potential process compromise.
Strip unnecessary Linux capabilities from system binaries to prevent privilege escalation even if a binary is compromised.
Disable all unnecessary Ubuntu background services and network daemons (e.g., Avahi, Bluetooth if unused) to reduce potential entry points.
Mount critical system directories (/bin, /sbin, /usr) as read-only during normal operation to prevent unauthorized system modifications.
Harden the network stack via sysctl parameters (e.g., disabling IP forwarding, ICMP redirects, and enabling SYN cookies).
Blacklist unused kernel modules for USB storage and other external media to prevent 'BadUSB' or data exfiltration attacks.
Disable password-based authentication and root login for SSH; enforce the use of strong SSH keys and non-standard ports.
Use Integrity Measurement Architecture (IMA) and Extended Verification Module (EVM) to verify the integrity of files before execution.
Restrict software updates to only official, cryptographically signed repositories and PPAs to prevent the installation of malicious packages.

title: Cloud-Robot Communication Security
description: Security for data exchange between robot and cloud VLM.

items.description: Establish a bidirectional trust relationship using TLS 1.3, where both the robot and the cloud server must verify each other's certificates.
The private keys used for mTLS must be stored in a hardware security module (TPM 2.0 or TEE) and never be exportable to the OS.
All raw sensor data (video streams, voice) must be encrypted at the source (robot) and only decrypted at the authorized cloud endpoint.
Use cipher suites that support Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) to ensure that past communications remain secure even if the long-term server key is compromised.
All control commands issued by the cloud VLM must be digitally signed using a server-side HSM to prevent command injection or forgery.
Include a unique nonce and a high-precision timestamp in every message to prevent attackers from capturing and replaying valid commands.
Implement Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to restrict cloud-to-robot commands based on the user's authorization level (e.g., Admin, Operator, Viewer).
Set a strict Time-to-Live (TTL) for all real-time control commands; commands received after the threshold must be automatically discarded.
Establish a dedicated secure tunnel (e.g., WireGuard or IPsec VPN) between the robot's local network and the cloud VPC for all management traffic.
Utilize QUIC/HTTP-3 to minimize handshake latency and improve connection resilience during handovers between different network types (e.g., 5G to Wi-Fi).
Implement rate limiting on both the robot and cloud endpoints to mitigate the impact of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks.
The robot must enter a 'Safe State' (e.g., immediate stop or balanced posture) if the heartbeat signal from the cloud is lost for more than 200ms.
Apply local de-identification (e.g., blurring faces or removing PII) on the robot before transmitting vision data to the cloud to ensure privacy compliance.
Attach a cryptographic hash and signature to sensor metadata to prove the data's origin and ensure it hasn't been tampered with during transit.

title: Local Network Security
description: Inter-board communication and internal network isolation.

items.description: All communications between the x86 control board and NVIDIA AI board must be authenticated and encrypted using TLS 1.3 or HMAC-based mutual authentication.
Implement VLANs to isolate management traffic, sensor data streams, and actuator control commands into separate logical networks.
Use static IP assignments and hardcoded ARP bindings to prevent internal ARP spoofing or IP hijacking attacks between boards.
Enable SROS2 (DDS-Security) to provide authentication, access control, and encryption for all ROS 2 topics exchanged within the robot.
Implement a hardware-based dual-watchdog system that monitors both primary boards; if either fails to respond within 10ms, the robot must enter a safe state.
Boards must perform mutual integrity attestation at startup, verifying each other's firmware signatures and secure boot states.
Apply rate limiting to internal communication interfaces to prevent a compromised sub-module from flooding the internal network with junk data.
Physically secure internal Ethernet ports and implement logical whitelisting to block any unauthorized internal network connections.
Deploy a lightweight IDS to monitor internal Ethernet traffic for abnormal patterns, such as unauthorized port scanning or protocol violations.
Establish a secure, encrypted channel directly between the TEEs of the x86 and NVIDIA boards for exchanging sensitive cryptographic keys.
Utilize the Linux Audit Framework (auditd) to record security-sensitive system calls in real-time, including execve, ptrace, and setuid.
Log all sudo usage history, SSH login attempts, and any access or modification attempts to critical configuration files like /etc/shadow.
Record events where CPU/Memory usage exceeds predefined thresholds or when a process monopolizes resources to detect signs of DoS attacks.
Configure local log files as 'Append-Only' (using chattr +a) to prevent the modification or deletion of existing records.
Hash each log block using a key stored within the TEE and link them in a chain to immediately detect if logs are missing or reordered.
Forward logs immediately to a cloud SIEM or a remote secure server via an encrypted mTLS channel to prevent evidence destruction.
Maintain a high-precision timestamped correlation between the Command ID received from the Cloud VLM and the physical motor actuation.
Store signature metadata alongside logs to prove that vision/audio data sent to the cloud originated from authentic physical sensor hardware.
Record every instance of a hardware E-Stop trigger or software Fail-Safe activation, accompanied by a system state snapshot.
Monitor for abnormal behaviors such as SSH access during off-hours, repeated authentication failures, or massive data transfers.
For critical violations (e.g., chassis intrusion, key zeroization), trigger an immediate administrative alert within 5 seconds.
Upon suspicion of a breach, automatically dump volatile memory states and active process lists to secure storage for investigation.
